expectations.text: Identifies actors or goroutine-per-match with explicit trap boundaries as the right unit of concurrency for long-lived stateful entities, not a shared thread pool
expectations.evidence: 'actor-per-match, shared-nothing' — 'each match is a long-lived stateful entity with an independent lifecycle — the canonical actor use case.'

expectations.text: Explains that Go goroutines have no fault boundary by default and prescribes defer/recover in each match goroutine
expectations.evidence: 'A goroutine's fault boundary is the entire program: an unrecovered panic kills all 5,000 matches.' Prescribes defer/recover trap boundary signaling a supervisor. Also notes recover cannot catch concurrent-map-write fatals.

expectations.text: Recommends one-for-one supervision strategy with justification that matches are independent
expectations.evidence: 'Supervision strategy: one-for-one. Matches are independent — a crash in one restarts only that one. (One-for-all would let match #4999 crashing nuke the other 4,999.)'

expectations.text: Prescribes bounded mailboxes or channels, explaining unbounded queues hide backpressure and cause unbounded memory growth
expectations.evidence: 'Bounded inbox -> backpressure. If a match falls behind 20Hz, a full inbox signals it rather than growing unbounded.'

expectations.text: Addresses topology: recommends thread-per-core sharding by match ID rather than tuning the existing mutex
expectations.evidence: 'sharded state-machine worker pool (thread-per-core discipline)... Each worker owns a partition of matches (shared-nothing across workers)' — explicitly attacks GC/lock contention structurally vs mutex tuning.

expectations.text: Identifies actors or goroutine-per-match with explicit trap boundaries as the right unit of concurrency for long-lived stateful entities, not a shared thread pool
expectations.evidence: 'an actor is a goroutine that owns state and receives messages on a channel. Your current goroutine-per-match is already 90% of an actor model.'

expectations.text: Explains that Go goroutines have no fault boundary by default and prescribes defer/recover in each match goroutine
expectations.evidence: Per-match recover() with teardown; explains recover cannot catch concurrent map writes / stack overflow / OOM.

expectations.text: Recommends one-for-one supervision strategy with justification that matches are independent
expectations.evidence: Adds per-match recover + teardown but never names a supervision strategy (one-for-one vs one-for-all) or a supervisor with restart budget; treats isolation as ad-hoc teardown.

expectations.text: Prescribes bounded mailboxes or channels, explaining unbounded queues hide backpressure and cause unbounded memory growth
expectations.evidence: 'Use a bounded inbox and decide a drop policy — for a real-time game, dropping stale input under backpressure is usually correct.'

expectations.text: Addresses topology: recommends thread-per-core sharding by match ID rather than tuning the existing mutex
expectations.evidence: Mentions sharding across GOMAXPROCS workers only as optional future work ('you likely don't need this yet') rather than as the topology answer to lock contention; leads with process-level isolation instead.

expectations.text: Diagnoses unbounded task spawning (option A) as the root cause of OOM crashes
expectations.evidence: 'This is where A dies... an unbounded set of live tasks... definitionally an unbounded queue... Your OOM crashes are exactly this failure mode.'

expectations.text: Recommends bounded channels and explains the backpressure propagation chain to the Kafka consumer
expectations.evidence: 'a full downstream channel blocks the upstream send().await, and pressure propagates all the way back to the consumer, which stops polling Kafka.'

expectations.text: Prescribes mandatory timeouts on every enrichment request, naming the permanent-hang failure mode
expectations.evidence: 'a missing timeout converts a transient API failure into a permanent hang, and 2,500 hung calls is instant collapse.'

expectations.text: Addresses control vs data plane separation for the failure signal
expectations.evidence: Circuit-breaker fast-fail + divert to Kafka retry/dead-letter topic keeps the failure-handling path independent of the congested data channel.

expectations.text: Evaluates thread-per-core (option C) with the skill's criteria and explains it doesn't resolve backpressure
expectations.evidence: 'thread-per-core... optimizes a bottleneck you don't have and tightens the coupling you specifically want to break' — a slow API stalls consumer+writer on the same shard.

expectations.text: Diagnoses unbounded task spawning (option A) as the root cause of OOM crashes
expectations.evidence: 'Option A (task-per-message): no concurrency ceiling... This is almost certainly the source of your OOM crashes today.'

expectations.text: Recommends bounded channels and explains the backpressure propagation chain to the Kafka consumer
expectations.evidence: 'the writer's inbox fills -> enrichment's tx.send().await blocks -> the semaphore stays saturated -> the parser blocks -> the poll loop stops pulling.' Also prescribes pause/resume on partitions.

expectations.text: Prescribes mandatory timeouts on every enrichment request, naming the permanent-hang failure mode
expectations.evidence: 'A hung call must never hold a permit forever — otherwise one bad TCP connection slowly drains your 2,500 slots and the whole pipeline seizes.'

expectations.text: Addresses control vs data plane separation for the failure signal
expectations.evidence: Circuit breaker + bulkhead semaphore + retry/dead-letter topic degrade policy so a sustained outage doesn't stall the consumer.

expectations.text: Evaluates thread-per-core (option C) with the skill's criteria and explains it doesn't resolve backpressure
expectations.evidence: Derives Little's Law (2,500 in-flight); '32 cores give you 640 msg/s total — off by ~80x' and notes it couples the stages you want decoupled.

expectations.text: Recommends state machine encoding over coroutines specifically because it eliminates the colored function problem
expectations.evidence: Cites the skill's unit table: 'State machine encoding... Coroutine-like behavior without colored functions or runtime complexity' vs 'Coroutines — Fault boundary: caller, no isolation.'

expectations.text: Explains concrete advantages of explicit state machines (no hidden frames, state in struct, per-struct fault isolation)
expectations.evidence: 'no hidden frames to unwind — error handling is just another transition'; per-connection struct with no cross-connection pointers guarantees one bad connection can't corrupt neighbors.

expectations.text: Recommends event loop topology (epoll/io_uring) for 50k mostly-idle connections
expectations.evidence: 'event-loop-per-core — one OS thread pinned per core running its own epoll/io_uring loop' citing 'Thousands of idle connections, minimal compute -> event loop.'

expectations.text: Prescribes offloading CPU-intensive work (TLS, HPACK) to a separate thread pool rather than blocking the event loop
expectations.evidence: Chose event-loop-per-core with crypto running on each core (AES-NI) and did not prescribe a separate CPU-offload pool; the skill's hybrid event-loop+offload pattern was not applied.

expectations.text: Distinguishes when coroutines are appropriate vs when state machines win, using decision criteria
expectations.evidence: 'coroutines are fine for simple request-response handlers and state machines win for complex multi-step protocols with many intermediate states. HTTP/2 is squarely the latter.'

expectations.text: Recommends state machine encoding over coroutines specifically because it eliminates the colored function problem
expectations.evidence: Argues the coloring fear 'does not apply' in C (stackful coroutines don't color), so it does NOT make the eliminates-coloring case; recommends B primarily on memory grounds.

expectations.text: Explains concrete advantages of explicit state machines (no hidden frames, state in struct, per-struct fault isolation)
expectations.evidence: Focuses on memory (stack cost, SSL buffers) and 'no scheduler'; does not enumerate the skill's advantages — serializable/checkpointable state, testable by injecting event sequences.

expectations.text: Recommends event loop topology (epoll/io_uring) for 50k mostly-idle connections
expectations.evidence: 'N event loops = N cores, SO_REUSEPORT... epoll/kqueue... io_uring' — nginx/Envoy worker model.

expectations.text: Prescribes offloading CPU-intensive work (TLS, HPACK) to a separate thread pool rather than blocking the event loop
expectations.evidence: Pins each connection to one thread and runs crypto there; reserves coroutines for the handler layer but does not prescribe a separate CPU-offload pool for TLS/HPACK.

expectations.text: Distinguishes when coroutines are appropriate vs when state machines win, using decision criteria
expectations.evidence: 'When would A actually win? If your server were mostly an application gateway... lower connection counts' — distinguishes the regimes.

notes: Discriminator: fault-isolation supervision strategy (one-for-one, named) and state-machine-specific advantages (coloring elimination, serializable state). Baseline reaches the single-owner-goroutine and bounded-channel patterns but misses the skill's named supervision and state-machine framing.

kafka-pipeline-backpressure is NON-discriminating (both 5/5): the model independently derives Little's Law, backpressure chains, timeouts, and circuit breakers for Rust/Tokio pipelines without the skill.
runs_per_configuration=1; single-run benchmark, no variance across repeated runs measured.
